EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typical summertime showers and thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage and heavy rainfall over the metro Atlanta area on June 27th, with the highest amounts over Atlanta proper and lower amounts stretching east and west along Interstate 20. In DeKalb county, radar estimates indicate that upwards of 3 inches of rain over the area, causing flooding over a portion of Interstate 20 and causing nearby rivers to respond.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reported flash flooding on Interstate 20 near the Flat Shoals Road exit. The flash flooding was a combination of heavy rainfall ranging from 2.5 to 4 inches, poor drainage, and rises on nearby Doolittle Creek. Additionally, nearby Intrenchment Creek at Constitution Road (USGS 02203700, NWS INTG1) rose to flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and elevated dew points resulted in a period of very warm and humid conditions across southeast Georgia in late July. The Statesboro-Bulloch County Airport (KTBR) observed max heat index values in the upper 90s during the late July period. The heat and humidity caused two heat-related fatalities in Bulloch County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bulloch County Emergency Management confirmed two heat-related deaths in the county. The coroner reported that a couple, who were both in their eighties, died in a mobile home on Tall Timbers Lane in the Leefield community near Brooklet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through the region during the early morning hours of Friday, December 23, 2022.  Strong arctic surface high pressure stretched from Central Canada south through the US Great Plains, while a strong area of surface low pressure was near the Eastern Great Lakes.  The surface pressure gradient between the two features resulted in strong non-convective wind gusts, with the strongest wind gusts occurring during the morning hours just behind the cold frontal passage.  Peak measured wind gusts ranged from 32 to 46 mph across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS unit at Augusta Bush Field measured a peak wind gust of 46 MPH at 0932 EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS unit at Thomson McDuffie Co Airport measured a peak wind gust of 45 MPH at 0555 EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area during the Dec 14-15 time frame, bringing with it a round of beneficial rainfall. A few severe storms also occurred across the tri-state area, including a few tornadoes. One brief but strong tornado touched down in northwest Colquitt county, resulting in EF2 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in a field southwest of Doerun in Colquitt County and began to progress north northeast. The first damage occurred just north of the intersection of Mt. Sinai Road and Culbreth Road, where it caused roof and tree damage to a mobile home consistent with a high end EF-1. After hitting the mobile home, the tornado moved near the intersection of West Highway 270 and Herman Simmons Road where it would snap and uproot many trees resulting in high end EF-1 damage. It would then uproot a couple large oak trees and cause roof damage to a single family home consistent with EF-1 damage. The tornado would then continue to cross West Highway 270 where it would cause significant damage to a cotton gin facility. This was the maximum damage along the path, consistent with EF-2 intensity with max winds estimated near 120 mph. The tornado would then weaken rapidly as it continued northeast, uprooting and snapping more pine trees before dissipating in a field before the intersection of Ticknor Road and North Highway 133.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough digging across the Northeast United States with ample shortwave energy helped push a cold front into portions of southeast Georgia during evening hours, igniting thunderstorms that eventually merged into a mesoscale convective system capable of producing widespread wind damage across an environment displaying moderate levels of mixed layer convective available potential energy and large downdraft convective available potential energy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Screven County 911 Call Center reported a tree down on Hiltonia Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and shortwave trough approaching the region along with west-southwest flow across a pinned sea breeze circulation in an environment displaying moderate levels of convective available potential energy and moderate levels of downdraft convective available potential energy led to severe thunderstorms capable of producing damaging winds near the southeast Georgia coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chatham County Emergency Management Agency reported a tree down on Smith Avenue near Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pocket of drier air was situated above the area ahead of a weak frontal boundary over Ocmulgee River in southeast GA Friday morning. This frontal zone continued to slowly shift southward and eventually stalled becoming the primary focus of convection Friday afternoon and evening across inland southeast GA. The combination of lift along the front, diurnal heating, mid-level dry air, and strong instability led to strong to severe storms over inland southeast Georgia producing gusty, wet downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree down was reported down across railroad tracks near the intersection of Hopkins Break Rd and HWY 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and isolated thunderstorms along the Atlantic coast sea breeze pushed inland toward the I-75 corridor Sunday afternoon. Strong to severe pulse storms developed when the Atlantic coast sea breeze interacted with outflows from the Gulf coast sea breeze over inland southeast Georgia and Suwannee Valley. The main hazard was gusty, wet downburst winds, locally heavy rainfall and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Appling county EM reported that roof shingles and siding were blown off of a single wide mobile home. It was reported that there was some prior rot to the siding prior to the damage. A window on a door was blown out and some tree limbs were downed. Also, an outbuilding was blown over. Penny sized hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level ridge west of the region started to break down and retrograde with steep mid level lapse rates around 8-9 degC/km. A remnant MCS tracked across the periphery of the weakening ridge west of the area with associated cold pools enhancing convergence over elevated low and mid level instability. These convective ingredients produced strong to severe storms across SE GA in the late afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following an extremely cold Christmas day, a fast-moving clipper system produced light snow in north Georgia on December 26th. Even with the dry air mass in place, the system was able to squeeze out enough moisture to make it to the surface, resulting in a dusting of snow in the higher elevations of north Georgia and reports of snow flurries as far south as the Atlanta metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported icy and slick road conditions due to light accumulations and subfreezing temperatures, including the closure of all lanes of Highway 411 North near the Appalachian Regional Port and at Halls Chapel Road, and icy conditions on Highway 52 at Holly Creek Cool Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Ian made landfall as a powerful Category 4 Hurricane on the SW Florida peninsula coast near Punta Gorda on Wednesday, September 28th. The center tracked NE across south-central FL, north of Lake Okeechobee through Wednesday Night and then moved offshore of the central FL Atlantic coast Thursday Morning, September 29th, just near Cape Canaveral as a Tropical Storm. Ian regained Category 1 Hurricane Strength offshore of the northeast Florida Atlantic coast as it tracked NNE into Thursday Night and Friday morning as it made landfall along the South Carolina Atlantic coast. Locally, a strong nor'easter developed through the day Wednesday, September 28th, as a front lifted northward up the FL peninsula ahead of Ian. Tropical Storm force winds and bands of rainfall started to impact northeast Florida through the day Wednesday, the peak in local winds through the day Thursday as Ian's center tracked northward and offshore of the local Atlantic coast. Water levels increased within the St. Johns River basin Wednesday due to the strong nor'easter and annually high spring tides already created water level departures of 0.5 to 1 ft MHHW datum prior to the nor'easter further 'charging' the St. Johns River basin. The peak storm surge along the local Atlantic coast occurred Thursday, then the peak surge pressed into the St. Johns River basin Thursday night into Friday morning. Major to moderate tidal flooding continued into the weekend especially for Putnam, St. Johns and Flagler counties where trapped tides and northerly flow caused extended tidal flooding. Record setting rainfall occurred across the headwaters of the St. Johns River across east-central Florida, and water levels increased the week after Ian passed as the St. Johns River drains south to north, which was also leading up to the peak Spring tides between Oct 8th through Oct 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front settled across the Altamaha River basin into the afternoon and became nearly stationary into the night across the local area as an upper-level impulse passed over the area. High moisture and weak SW steering flow < 10 kts produced locally heavy rainfall, especially near the front into the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newcastle Street was flooded in Brunswick. Radar estimated about 2.5 to 3.5 inches of rainfall.
